title: Environmental Triggers

content.question: What's one environmental tweak that would nudge you toward the habit you want?
content.equation: Net Cues = Good Cues − Bad Cues
content.life_lens: Water in view; floss by sink.
sop: Walk your space and list every visible cue that currently triggers an unwanted behavior (phone in sightline, snacks on the counter)
Remove or hide the two worst cues today
Place one good cue in the most obvious sightline for the habit you want (water bottle on the desk, shoes by the door)
Run it for one week before adding a second cue
Re-audit the space monthly, cues stop working once your eyes stop registering them
content.explainer: Behavior isn't mostly a willpower problem, it's a response to whatever is physically visible and easy to reach in the moment. Change what's in front of you and the behavior tends to follow without a fight; leave the old cues in place and no amount of motivation reliably beats them twice a day.
questions: What's one environmental tweak that would nudge you toward the habit you want?
Which object in your space is triggering a behavior you're actively trying to stop?
If someone studied only the cues in your workspace, which habit would they bet you're building, is it the right one?
content.equation_example: Count the cues in your environment: 3 supportive cues in your sightline, 1 remaining bad cue. Net Cues = 3 − 1 = 2. Higher (more good, fewer bad) means the behavior happens with less willpower; negative means your environment still defaults you back to the old behavior.
content.closing_statement: Design the room so willpower isn't required.

caption:
Principle Nº 207: Environmental Triggers

Design space to cue success.

Ask yourself: What's one environmental tweak that would nudge you toward the habit you want?
